exploit_chains.name: Physical Memory Attack
steps: 1. Use phys-rw (IOCTL 0x81112F08 [phys mem map], 0x81113004 [phys mem map], 0x81113008 [phys mem map] +1 more) to scan physical memory for EPROCESS signatures
2. Locate current process EPROCESS in physical memory
3. Overwrite Token field with SYSTEM process token
4. Alternative: map ntoskrnl .text, patch code inline

findings.title: Integer overflow before ExAllocatePoolWithTag at 0x14002BC33
findings.description: Arithmetic (imul/shl/mul) at 0x14002BC1C precedes ExAllocatePoolWithTag call at 0x14002BC33 with no overflow guard (no jo/jb between them). If the multiplied value is user-controlled, this causes a heap overflow.

findings.title: Double-fetch (TOCTOU) at 0x1400015B3
findings.description: Two reads from [reg+0x18] at 0x14000150A and 0x1400015B3 with a cmp/test using the first value and the second value flowing into a sensitive sink (allocator / memcpy / deref). An attacker can race to change the buffer between check and use.

findings.title: No PreviousMode / RequestorMode check detected
findings.description: Driver does not import ExGetPreviousMode or KeGetPreviousMode and no inline KTHREAD.PreviousMode read was found. Without checking PreviousMode, a kernel-mode caller could be impersonated, or user-mode requests may bypass validation. IOCTLs should verify RequestorMode == UserMode before trusting input.

findings.title: IOCTL handlers missing NULL buffer check
No privilege check APIs imported
findings.description: Driver handles IOCTLs but does not import SeSinglePrivilegeCheck, SePrivilegeCheck, or SeAccessCheck. Sensitive operations should verify caller privileges beyond just device handle access rights.

findings.title: IoCreateDevice without FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN
findings.description: Driver uses IoCreateDevice instead of IoCreateDeviceSecure and FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag was not detected. Without this flag, the device object inherits permissive default security, allowing any user to open a handle. Use IoCreateDeviceSecure with an SDDL string to restrict access.

findings.title: Signing certificate EXPIRED (valid until 2024-05-10T23:59:59+00:00)
findings.description: The code-signing certificate has expired. While drivers with a valid timestamp counter-signature remain valid after expiry, an expired cert without a timestamp means the signature is no longer trusted. This is also common in BYOVD — old vulnerable drivers with expired certs that were signed before revocation.

findings.title: Expired certificates in chain: USERTrust RSA Certification Authority
findings.description: One or more certificates in the signing chain have expired. If the root or intermediate CA cert has expired, the entire chain of trust is broken unless a valid timestamp exists.

findings.title: PKCS#7 signature verifies cryptographically
findings.description: The primary Authenticode PKCS#7 signature was verified against the signer certificate's public key.

findings.title: Authenticode PE hash matches (binary not tampered)
findings.description: Recomputed sha256 hash of the PE image matches the value embedded in SpcIndirectDataContent.

findings.title: Nested signature(s) present: sha256
findings.description: The primary signature is dual-signed — a second (typically SHA-256) signature is nested via MsSpcNestedSignature. Windows on modern Windows 10+ / HVCI prefers the SHA-256 side for verification.

findings.title: Chain anchored to kernel-trusted root (ms-root-referenced)
findings.description: Chain terminates at 'Microsoft Code Verification Root' — recognised as kernel-trusted (ms-root-referenced).
